## Items Added (4 total)

### 1. X — AI operating system, not a chat tool
- **Platform:** Twitter / X (`@jeditrinupab`, account 16969)
- **Scheduled:** 2026-05-14 08:31 ICT (01:31 UTC)
- **Status:** `ready_for_review`
- **Source angle:** LF1 — AI as operating system (DeployCo / OpenAI)
- **ID:** `blaze-daily-2026-05-14-x1-*`

**Post text:**
> Most teams use AI like a faster search engine.
>
> Ask a question. Get an answer. Move on.
>
> The teams pulling ahead are doing something different.
>
> They built an operating system:
> — which tasks AI handles without a human
> — which outputs need review before going live
> — which decisions stay with the founder
>
> Old way: everyone uses ChatGPT however they want.
> New way: the business has a workflow, not just a chatbox.
>
> The difference shows up in margins, not demos.

---

### 2. LinkedIn — The 10-80-10 rule for AI in business
- **Platform:** LinkedIn (Jet personal profile, account 19173)
- **Scheduled:** 2026-05-14 10:00 ICT (03:00 UTC)
- **Status:** `ready_for_review`
- **Source angle:** LF1 — AI Operating System / DeployCo + 10-80-10 rule
- **ID:** `blaze-daily-2026-05-14-li1-*`

**Post text:**
> OpenAI just launched a dedicated deployment company.
>
> Not to build a better chatbot. To help businesses move AI from experiment to production.
>
> That gap — from 'we tried it' to 'it runs reliably' — is the real competitive gap right now.
>
> Most businesses I talk to are stuck at the experiment stage. The team uses ChatGPT in different ways. Quality varies by person. The founder cannot measure what is working or why.
>
> The businesses pulling ahead solved it with a simple rule: 10-80-10.
>
> • 10% — the founder or team lead sets direction and defines what good looks like
> • 80% — AI handles the execution: drafts, summaries, analysis, follow-ups
> • 10% — a human reviews and approves before anything goes to a customer
>
> This is not automation. It is workflow design.
>
> The shift is small. The output difference is not.
>
> Three workflows worth redesigning first, for most SMEs:
> 1. Lead qualification and first response
> 2. Meeting summaries into next actions
> 3. Proposal or quote generation
>
> Pick one. Build the SOP. Test it for two weeks.
>
> That is more useful than evaluating the next new tool.

---

### 3. X — Your customers already use ChatGPT
- **Platform:** Twitter / X (`@jeditrinupab`, account 16969)
- **Scheduled:** 2026-05-14 12:31 ICT (05:31 UTC)
- **Status:** `ready_for_review`
- **Source angle:** LF2 — 35+ ChatGPT adoption / customer expectation shift
- **ID:** `blaze-daily-2026-05-14-x2-*`

**Post text:**
> Your customers are using ChatGPT before they call your sales team.
>
> They compare prices.
> They summarize reviews.
> They arrive with sharper questions.
>
> If your FAQ is thin, your admin replies slowly, and your follow-up is generic — you look slow next to an AI.
>
> This is not a threat. It is a design problem.
>
> Three things to fix:
> 1. A clear FAQ that answers the real objections
> 2. A comparison page that is honest about trade-offs
> 3. Sales summaries: 5-line customer context before every call
>
> The buyer has upgraded. The business can too.

---

### 4. X — Brief before spend, not boost before thinking
- **Platform:** Twitter / X (`@jeditrinupab`, account 16969)
- **Scheduled:** 2026-05-14 20:31 ICT (13:31 UTC)
- **Status:** `ready_for_review`
- **Source angle:** LF3 — AI ad brief / Google Small Brief
- **ID:** `blaze-daily-2026-05-14-x3-*`

**Post text:**
> Most ad budget is wasted before the campaign even starts.
>
> Not because the platform is wrong.
> Because the brief is weak.
>
> Old way:
> Photo → price → discount → boost.
>
> Better way:
> — Who is the customer and what actually hurts them?
> — What makes this offer different from every alternative?
> — Why buy now, not later?
>
> AI can generate 10 campaign angles in 5 minutes.
> The founder's job is to pick the one that is actually true.
>
> Clear brief = better creative = less money wasted testing obvious failures.
